schools.summary: Rachel's envy and impatient cry: 'and when Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister; and said unto Jacob, Give me children, or else I die' — Rachel, barren while Leah bore four sons (29:31-35), envying her sister (the sin of envy and discontent, the beloved wife, 29:30, grieved at her barrenness, the rivalry of the sisters breaking out); Rachel saying to Jacob, 'Give me children, or else I die' — an impatient, fretful, immoderate demand: her heart set inordinately on children (one comfort lacking embittered all the rest, the discontent that could not be satisfied, the impatience that, as it were, would die without children, Henry noting, 'we are very apt to be discontented with what God has appointed us,' the fretting of the envious heart, demanding of Jacob what only God could give, v.2, the sinful, immoderate craving, contrast Hannah's prayer, 1 Samuel 1:10-11, who took her grief to God); Rachel demanding children of Jacob ('Give me children'), as if her husband could give what God withholds (the misdirected craving, Jacob rightly answering, Am I in God's stead? v.2, the discontent that looked to man, not God, the lesson that children are the gift of God, Psalm 127:3, to be sought of him) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

debates.question: What does Rachel's 'Give me children, or else I die' display?

sides.position: The envy of her sister and the impatient, immoderate demand of a discontented heart - set inordinately on children, demanding of Jacob what only God could give (v.2; Psalm 127:3); the misdirected craving (contrast Hannah, 1 Samuel 1)

themes: And when Rachel saw that she bare Jacob no children, Rachel envied her sister; and said unto Jacob, Give me children, or else I die
Rachel's envy and impatient cry (the crux of her discontent)
the immoderate, fretful demand of the discontented heart
demanding of Jacob what only God could give (v.2; Psalm 127:3)

schools.summary: Jacob's reply that children are the gift of God: 'and Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel: and he said, Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?' — Jacob displeased at Rachel's fretful, misdirected demand (v.1, the just, if not wholly patient, displeasure at her impatience, the husband grieved that she demanded of him what was God's to give, the rebuke of her discontent); Jacob's answer — 'Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?': Jacob disclaiming the power to give children (he is not God, it is God, not man, who opens and shuts the womb, 29:31; 1 Samuel 1:5, God having withheld children from Rachel, the children God's gift, Psalm 127:3, to be sought of him, not demanded of the husband, Jacob's reply, though sharp, rightly directing Rachel to God, the acknowledgment that fruitfulness is in God's hand alone); God having withheld the fruit of the womb from Rachel (the sovereign hand of God in giving and withholding children, the lesson for Rachel, and all, to seek of God, in prayer, as she at last did, v.22, God remembered Rachel, what God alone can give, the gift of children in God's hand) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel: and he said, Am I in God's stead, who hath withheld from thee the fruit of the womb?
Jacob's reply that children are the gift of God
God alone opens and shuts the womb (29:31; Psalm 127:3)
directing Rachel to seek of God what he withholds (v.22)

schools.summary: Rachel gives Bilhah to bear children for her: 'and she said, Behold my maid Bilhah, go in unto her; and she shall bear upon my knees, that I may also have children by her' — Rachel, still barren, giving her handmaid Bilhah to Jacob to bear children for her (as Sarah gave Hagar, 16:2-3, the expedient of the time, the children of the handmaid reckoned to the mistress, the impatient resort to human means, rather than waiting on God, who would remember her, v.22, the custom followed); 'she shall bear upon my knees' — a token of adoption (the child born on the knees of the mistress, reckoned as hers, Rachel claiming the handmaid's children as her own, the legal fiction of the time, the children counted to Rachel); 'that I may also have children by her' (Rachel seeking children, even by the handmaid, to match Leah, the rivalry of the sisters, the impatient expedient, as Sarah's, 16:2, which the witnesses note was not without fault, yet overruled by God, the children of Bilhah, Dan, Naphtali, vv.5-8, reckoned to Rachel) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And she said, Behold my maid Bilhah, go in unto her; and she shall bear upon my knees, that I may also have children by her
Rachel giving Bilhah to bear children for her
the impatient resort to the handmaid (as Sarah, 16:2-3), the children reckoned to Rachel
the rivalry of the sisters continued

schools.summary: Rachel gives Bilhah to Jacob: 'and she gave him Bilhah her handmaid to wife: and Jacob went in unto her' — Rachel giving Bilhah to Jacob as a (secondary) wife/concubine, and Jacob taking her (as Abraham took Hagar, 16:3-4, the carrying out of Rachel's expedient, v.3, the handmaid given to bear children for the mistress, the act done); Bilhah given 'to wife' (a secondary wife/concubine, as Hagar, 16:3, the children to be reckoned to Rachel, v.3, the customary arrangement, the handmaid become a mother of the tribes, Dan, Naphtali, vv.6-8); 'Jacob went in unto her' (Jacob taking Bilhah, the conception to follow, v.5, the providence, and the human expedient, building the house of Israel, the handmaid's children among the twelve) — Gill.

themes: And she gave him Bilhah her handmaid to wife: and Jacob went in unto her
Rachel giving Bilhah to Jacob
the handmaid given as a secondary wife (as Hagar, 16:3)
to bear children for Rachel (vv.6-8)

schools.summary: Bilhah bears Jacob a son: 'and Bilhah conceived, and bare Jacob a son' — Bilhah (Rachel's handmaid) conceiving and bearing Jacob a son (Dan, v.6, the first child of the handmaid, the children reckoned to Rachel, v.3, the building of Jacob's house by the handmaid, the providence, through the expedient, multiplying the seed); Bilhah conceiving at once — the handmaid fruitful (while Rachel was barren, the child to be Rachel's, by adoption, v.3, the conception that began the second round of births); 'bare Jacob a son' (the first of Bilhah's two sons, Dan, Naphtali, vv.6-8, the son reckoned to Rachel, the handmaid a mother of the tribes, the house of Israel growing) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Bilhah conceived, and bare Jacob a son
Bilhah bearing Jacob a son
the handmaid's first son (Dan, v.6), reckoned to Rachel (v.3)
the house of Israel growing through the handmaid

schools.summary: The naming of Dan, God having judged Rachel: 'and Rachel said, God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice, and hath given me a son: therefore called she his name Dan' — Rachel naming the son Dan ('judging,' or 'he hath judged'), because God had judged her cause (vindicated her, given sentence in her favor) and heard her prayer (the acknowledgment that God had regarded her, the name commemorating God's judging/hearing); 'God hath judged me' (God having given sentence in Rachel's favor, judged her cause against her barrenness, or against Leah's reproaching, freeing her from the reproach, the divine vindication, Rachel owning God's hand, yet the child by the handmaid, the judging God); 'hath also heard my voice' (God having heard Rachel's prayer, so she had prayed, amid her impatience, the answered cry, the name Dan, 'judge,' commemorating God's judging and hearing, the son who would head the tribe of Dan) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Rachel said, God hath judged me, and hath also heard my voice, and hath given me a son: therefore called she his name Dan
the naming of Dan, God having judged Rachel
God judging her cause (vindicating her) and hearing her prayer
the son who would head the tribe of Dan ('judge')

schools.summary: Bilhah bears a second son: 'and Bilhah Rachel's maid conceived again, and bare Jacob a second son' — Bilhah conceiving again and bearing Jacob a second son (Naphtali, v.8, the second child of the handmaid, reckoned to Rachel, v.3, the house of Israel growing, the providence multiplying the seed through the handmaid); Bilhah conceiving again — the handmaid fruitful a second time (the child reckoned to Rachel, the second of Bilhah's two sons); 'bare Jacob a second son' (Naphtali, v.8, the second son of the handmaid, the tribe of Naphtali, the gathering of the twelve) — Gill.

themes: And Bilhah Rachel's maid conceived again, and bare Jacob a second son
Bilhah bearing a second son
the second child of the handmaid (Naphtali, v.8), reckoned to Rachel
the house of Israel growing

schools.summary: The naming of Naphtali, Rachel's wrestlings with her sister: 'and Rachel said, With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called his name Naphtali' — Rachel naming the son Naphtali ('my wrestling,' or 'wrestlings of God,' i.e., great/mighty wrestlings, the 'wrestlings' expressing the contest, rivalry, with Leah for children and her husband's favor, Rachel claiming to have prevailed, by the two sons of Bilhah, the name commemorating the struggle); the Hebrew ('wrestlings of God') may mean great/mighty wrestlings (the divine name used as a superlative), or wrestlings before God (in prayer, the great contest, the rivalry, possibly joined with prayer, the struggle of the sisters); 'I have prevailed' (Rachel reckoning she had prevailed in the contest with Leah, by Bilhah's sons, the spirit of rivalry, not wholly commendable, the struggle for children and favor, the name Naphtali commemorating the wrestling, the tribe of Naphtali) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Rachel said, With great wrestlings have I wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed: and she called his name Naphtali
the naming of Naphtali, Rachel's wrestlings with her sister
the rivalry with Leah for children and favor
Naphtali ('my wrestling'), the name commemorating the struggle

schools.summary: Leah gives Zilpah to bear children: 'when Leah saw that she had left bearing, she took Zilpah her maid, and gave her Jacob to wife' — Leah, having ceased bearing (after four sons, 29:35), and seeing Rachel's expedient (vv.3-8), giving her own handmaid Zilpah to Jacob to bear more children (the rivalry continued, Leah matching Rachel's resort to the handmaid, the impatient expedient); Leah giving Zilpah (her handmaid, 29:24) to Jacob — to bear children reckoned to Leah (the same expedient as Rachel's, v.3, the rivalry of the sisters, the handmaid given); 'gave her Jacob to wife' (Zilpah given as a secondary wife, as Bilhah, v.4, the children, Gad, Asher, vv.10-13, reckoned to Leah, the contest for children carried on, the house of Israel growing through the handmaids, the rivalry, not commendable, yet overruled) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: When Leah saw that she had left bearing, she took Zilpah her maid, and gave her Jacob to wife
Leah giving Zilpah to bear children
matching Rachel's expedient (vv.3-8), the rivalry continued
the children (Gad, Asher) reckoned to Leah

schools.summary: Zilpah bears Jacob a son: 'and Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a son' — Zilpah (Leah's handmaid) bearing Jacob a son (Gad, v.11, the first child of Leah's handmaid, reckoned to Leah, v.9, the house of Israel growing, the providence multiplying the seed); Zilpah bearing for Leah — the handmaid's child reckoned to the mistress (the rivalry's fruit, the second handmaid become a mother of the tribes, Gad, Asher, vv.11-13); 'bare Jacob a son' (the first of Zilpah's two sons, Gad, v.11, the son reckoned to Leah, the tribe of Gad, the gathering of the twelve) — Gill, Keil and Delitzsch.

themes: And Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a son
Zilpah bearing Jacob a son
the first child of Leah's handmaid (Gad, v.11), reckoned to Leah
the house of Israel growing through the second handmaid

schools.summary: The naming of Gad: 'and Leah said, A troop cometh: and she called his name Gad' — Leah naming the son Gad ('a troop,' or 'good fortune,' the name, the Hebrew read two ways, commemorating either 'a troop cometh,' the increase of children, a company coming, or 'good fortune,' fortune is come, the sense of good luck/blessing, the joy at the added son); the two senses of 'Gad' — 'a troop/company' (the multiplying of children, a band coming) or 'fortune/good luck' (Ba-gad, 'with fortune,' or 'fortune is come,' the name expressing Leah's sense of increase and blessing, the joy at the son); 'she called his name Gad' (the son named Gad, a troop/fortune, the first of Zilpah's two sons, the tribe of Gad, the house of Israel growing) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Leah said, A troop cometh: and she called his name Gad
the naming of Gad
Gad ('a troop' or 'good fortune'), the joy at the added son
the tribe of Gad, the house of Israel growing

schools.summary: Zilpah bears a second son: 'and Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a second son' — Zilpah bearing Jacob a second son (Asher, v.13, the second child of Leah's handmaid, reckoned to Leah, v.9, the house of Israel growing, the providence multiplying the seed through the handmaid); Zilpah bearing again for Leah — the handmaid's second child (the rivalry's fruit, the second of Zilpah's two sons); 'bare Jacob a second son' (Asher, v.13, the second son of the handmaid, the tribe of Asher, the gathering of the twelve) — Gill.

themes: And Zilpah Leah's maid bare Jacob a second son
Zilpah bearing a second son
the second child of Leah's handmaid (Asher, v.13), reckoned to Leah
the house of Israel growing

schools.summary: The naming of Asher: 'and Leah said, Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed: and she called his name Asher' — Leah naming the son Asher ('happy,' or 'blessed,' the name commemorating her happiness/blessedness in the added son, 'the daughters, women, will call me blessed,' count me happy, as a fruitful mother, the joy at the son); Leah counting herself happy and blessed (the women would call her so, the joy of the fruitful mother, the sense of blessing, children a heritage of the LORD, Psalm 127:3, the happiness Leah found in her sons, cf. Luke 1:48, all generations shall call me blessed); 'she called his name Asher (happy/blessed)' (the son named for Leah's happiness, the second of Zilpah's two sons, the tribe of Asher, the house of Israel growing) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Leah said, Happy am I, for the daughters will call me blessed: and she called his name Asher
the naming of Asher
Asher ('happy/blessed'), the joy of the fruitful mother (Psalm 127:3)
the tribe of Asher, the house of Israel growing

schools.summary: The mandrakes and Rachel's request: 'and Reuben went in the days of wheat harvest, and found mandrakes in the field, and brought them unto his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, Give me, I pray thee, of thy son's mandrakes' — Reuben (Leah's eldest, a child) finding mandrakes (Hebrew dudaim) at wheat harvest and bringing them to Leah (the dudaim, 'love-apples,' variously identified: the mandrake, a plant whose fruit and root were anciently reputed to promote love and fertility, or some other fruit/flower, jasmine, citron, or fragrant blossoms, the witnesses noting the identification is uncertain, the plants prized, by some, for their supposed power to aid conception); the supposed virtue of the dudaim (to promote fertility) yet the witnesses careful: the mandrakes did not in fact give the children — for it was Leah (not Rachel) who, after the bargain, conceived (vv.17-18), while Rachel, who got the mandrakes, remained barren until 'God remembered Rachel' (v.22), thus the narrative pointedly showing that fruitfulness was of God, not of the mandrakes (the supposed remedy failing where God had not given, succeeding only where God gave, the witnesses warning against the superstition that ascribed conception to the plant, when it was God's gift, v.22; Psalm 127:3); Rachel, still barren, desiring the mandrakes (hoping, it seems, they might help her conceive — the very superstition the story refutes, the request that led to the bargain, v.15, and Leah's bearing, vv.16-18, the irony that Rachel got the mandrakes but not, thereby, the child) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What of the mandrakes (dudaim) and their supposed power?

sides.position: The 'love-apples,' anciently reputed to promote fertility - but the narrative refutes the superstition: Rachel, who got them, stayed barren till God remembered her (v.22), while Leah (without them) conceived (vv.17-18); fruitfulness is of God, not the plant (Psalm 127:3)

themes: And Reuben... found mandrakes in the field, and brought them unto his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, Give me, I pray thee, of thy son's mandrakes
the mandrakes and Rachel's request (the crux of the dudaim)
the 'love-apples' reputed to aid fertility, the identification uncertain
the narrative refuting the superstition - fruitfulness is of God, not the plant (Psalm 127:3; v.22)

schools.summary: The bargain over the mandrakes and the husband: 'and she said unto her, Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband? and wouldest thou take away my son's mandrakes also? And Rachel said, Therefore he shall lie with thee to night for thy son's mandrakes' — Leah complaining that Rachel had taken (engrossed) her husband's love (Jacob loving Rachel more, 29:30, and now would she take the mandrakes too? the bitterness of the unloved wife, 29:31, the rivalry over the husband's affection, the complaint of the slighted Leah); Leah's grievance — that Rachel had the chief place in Jacob's affection (29:30, the sad fruit of the rivalry, the unloved wife's bitterness, the contest for the husband); Rachel bargaining — Leah might have Jacob that night in exchange for the mandrakes (the sad spectacle of the sisters bargaining over their husband, the rivalry reduced to a trade, the bargain that, by God's overruling, led to Leah's conceiving Issachar, vv.16-18, the providence working even through the unseemly contest) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And she said unto her, Is it a small matter that thou hast taken my husband?... Therefore he shall lie with thee to night for thy son's mandrakes
the bargain over the mandrakes and the husband
the bitterness of the unloved wife and the rivalry over Jacob's affection (29:30-31)
the contest that, by God's overruling, led to Leah's conceiving (vv.16-18)

schools.summary: Leah meets Jacob, having hired him: 'and Jacob came out of the field in the evening, and Leah went out to meet him, and said, Thou must come in unto me; for surely I have hired thee with my son's mandrakes. And he lay with her that night' — Leah meeting Jacob and claiming him for the night (by the bargain, v.15, the unusual claim of the wife, the rivalry's strange fruit, the 'hiring' of the husband, the bargain carried out); the 'hiring' — Leah having bought Jacob's company for the night with the mandrakes (v.15, the sad reduction of the marriage to a bargain, the rivalry's unseemly fruit, yet the occasion of Issachar's conception, vv.17-18, whose name reflects the 'hire'); 'he lay with her that night' (Jacob complying, the bargain's result, God overruling the strange contest to give Leah another son, Issachar, vv.17-18, the providence working through the rivalry to build the house of Israel, the conception that followed) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And Jacob came out of the field in the evening, and Leah went out to meet him... for surely I have hired thee with my son's mandrakes. And he lay with her that night
Leah meeting Jacob, having hired him
the strange fruit of the rivalry, the 'hiring' of the husband
the providence overruling to give Leah another son (Issachar, vv.17-18)

schools.summary: God hearkens to Leah, who bears a fifth son: 'and God hearkened unto Leah, and she conceived, and bare Jacob the fifth son' — God hearing Leah (her prayer/desire) and giving her conception (the fifth son, Issachar, v.18, the conception ascribed to God, not the mandrakes, v.14 — for it was Leah, who gave up the mandrakes, who conceived, not Rachel who got them, the gift of God in answer to Leah); the conception of God ('God hearkened') — the pointed lesson that fruitfulness is God's gift (Psalm 127:3), not the mandrakes' (v.14, Leah's son the answer to her prayer, the providence, not the superstition, giving the child); 'bare Jacob the fifth son' (Issachar, v.18, Leah's fifth son, the tribe of Issachar, the house of Israel growing, the gift of God) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And God hearkened unto Leah, and she conceived, and bare Jacob the fifth son
God hearkening to Leah, who bears a fifth son
the conception of God, not the mandrakes (v.14; Psalm 127:3)
Issachar (v.18), the gift of God in answer to Leah

schools.summary: The naming of Issachar: 'and Leah said, God hath given me my hire, because I have given my maiden to my husband: and she called his name Issachar' — Leah naming the son Issachar ('he brings hire,' or 'there is reward/hire,' the name commemorating that God had given her a 'hire,' reward — either for giving her maid Zilpah to Jacob, v.9, or recalling the 'hiring' of Jacob with the mandrakes, v.16, the reward owned as God's gift); Leah's reckoning — that God had rewarded her for giving Zilpah to Jacob (v.9, the 'hire,' reward, ascribed to God, the witnesses noting the imperfect reasoning, yet the owning of God's gift, the name reflecting the reward); 'she called his name Issachar (hire/reward)' (the fifth son named for the reward, the tribe of Issachar, the house of Israel growing, the gift of God owned) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Leah said, God hath given me my hire, because I have given my maiden to my husband: and she called his name Issachar
the naming of Issachar
Issachar ('hire/reward'), the reward owned as God's gift
the tribe of Issachar, the house of Israel growing

schools.summary: Leah bears a sixth son: 'and Leah conceived again, and bare Jacob the sixth son' — Leah conceiving again and bearing Jacob a sixth son (Zebulun, v.20, the sixth of her own sons, the house of Israel growing, the fruitfulness of Leah, now six sons of her own, besides the two of Zilpah); Leah conceiving again — the continued fruitfulness of the once-unloved wife (29:31, the gift of God, the sixth son); 'bare Jacob the sixth son' (Zebulun, v.20, the sixth son of Leah, the tribe of Zebulun, the gathering of the twelve) — Gill.

themes: And Leah conceived again, and bare Jacob the sixth son
Leah bearing a sixth son
the continued fruitfulness of the once-unloved wife (29:31)
Zebulun (v.20), the house of Israel growing

schools.summary: The naming of Zebulun and Leah's hope: 'and Leah said, God hath endued me with a good dowry; now will my husband dwell with me, because I have born him six sons: and she called his name Zebulun' — Leah naming the son Zebulun ('dwelling,' from her hope her husband would dwell with her, there is also a play on a word for 'endued/gifted,' the 'good dowry,' the gift of six sons, owned as God's, the name commemorating both the gift and the hope); Leah's hope — that now, having borne six sons, her husband would 'dwell with' (cleave to, be attached to) her (the continued longing of the once-unloved wife for Jacob's affection, as with Reuben, Levi, 29:32-34, the hope renewed, the craving for the husband's love); 'she called his name Zebulun (dwelling)' (the sixth son named for the hope of dwelling, the tribe of Zebulun, the house of Israel growing, the gift of God and the hope of the wife) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Leah said, God hath endued me with a good dowry; now will my husband dwell with me... and she called his name Zebulun
the naming of Zebulun and Leah's hope of dwelling with her husband
the six sons owned as God's good gift
the continued longing of the once-unloved wife for Jacob's love (29:32-34)

schools.summary: The birth of Dinah: 'and afterwards she bare a daughter, and called her name Dinah' — Leah bearing a daughter, Dinah ('judgment,' the feminine of Dan, or 'judged/vindicated,' the only daughter of Jacob named in the record, he had other daughters, 37:35, but Dinah alone is named, for the history of ch. 34, the daughter whose later violation, ch. 34, the name perhaps anticipates); Leah bearing Dinah — a daughter (named, unlike Jacob's other daughters, for the history of ch. 34, the child of Leah, the household of Jacob); 'called her name Dinah (judgment)' (the daughter named Dinah, the name, perhaps anticipating her cause, ch. 34, the only named daughter, the household of Jacob complete, eleven sons and a daughter so far, before Benjamin) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And afterwards she bare a daughter, and called her name Dinah
the birth of Dinah
the only daughter of Jacob named in the record (for the history of ch. 34)
Dinah ('judgment')

schools.summary: God remembers Rachel and opens her womb: 'and God remembered Rachel, and God hearkened to her, and opened her womb' — 'God remembered Rachel' — not that God had forgotten her (he forgets none of his), but the anthropomorphism marking the effect produced: God now acting for Rachel, regarding her, and giving what he had long withheld ('the memory of God is named from the effect produced,' the turning-point — after all the rivalry, the handmaids, and the mandrakes, which gave nothing, v.14, God himself, in his own time, remembering the barren Rachel and giving her a child, the gift wholly of God); 'God hearkened to her' (Rachel having at last sought God by prayer, her early impatience, vv.1-2, giving way to prayer, her prayer and faith obtaining what envy and the mandrakes could not, the answered prayer, the lesson that what is sought of God, not of man or means, is given in his time, the prayer heard); 'opened her womb' (God giving Rachel conception, the barren made fruitful, as Sarah, Rebekah, and later Hannah, the gift of God alone, Psalm 127:3, the birth of Joseph, vv.23-24, the long-barren Rachel at last a mother, the providence in God's own time) — Clarke, Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

sides.position: The anthropomorphism marks that God now acted for her (he had not forgotten) - after the rivalry, handmaids, and mandrakes (which gave nothing, v.14), God himself, in his own time and in answer to prayer, gave the barren Rachel a child; the gift wholly of God (Psalm 127:3)

themes: And God remembered Rachel, and God hearkened to her, and opened her womb
God remembering Rachel and opening her womb (the crux of God's remembering)
the anthropomorphism marking God's acting for her (he had not forgotten)
the gift wholly of God, in his own time and in answer to prayer (after the mandrakes gave nothing, v.14; Psalm 127:3)

schools.summary: Rachel bears a son, her reproach taken away: 'and she conceived, and bare a son; and said, God hath taken away my reproach' — Rachel, remembered by God (v.22), conceiving and bearing a son (Joseph, v.24, the long-barren wife at last a mother, the gift of God, the joy of the answered prayer); Rachel saying 'God hath taken away my reproach' (the reproach of barrenness, childlessness was reckoned a reproach in Israel, as for Sarah, Rebekah, Hannah, Elisabeth, Luke 1:25, God having removed it by giving her a son, the relief and joy, the reproach lifted by God's gift); 'God hath taken away my reproach' (the barrenness, the reproach, removed by the gift of a son, God's hand owned, the joy of the once-barren wife, the birth that would be named Joseph, v.24) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And she conceived, and bare a son; and said, God hath taken away my reproach
Rachel bearing a son and her reproach taken away
the reproach of barrenness (Luke 1:25) removed by God's gift
the once-barren wife at last a mother

schools.summary: The naming of Joseph and Rachel's hope: 'and she called his name Joseph; and said, The LORD shall add to me another son' — Rachel naming the son Joseph ('he shall add,' or 'adding,' the name commemorating both the taking away of her reproach, v.23, by a play, 'asaph,' gathered/taken away, and the hope that the LORD would 'add,' yasaph, another son, the double sense in the name, the son Joseph, the beloved, the future deliverer of his house, chs. 37-50); Rachel's faith/hope — 'the LORD shall add to me another son' (the expectation of further blessing, fulfilled in Benjamin, 35:18, at whose birth she would die, the hope of the once-barren wife, the faith looking for more of God's gift); 'she called his name Joseph' (the son of Rachel, the beloved, the future saviour of Jacob's house in Egypt, chs. 37-50, a notable type, by many, of Christ, the beloved son, rejected by his brethren, exalted to save, the tribe(s) of Joseph, Ephraim and Manasseh, the gift of God to Rachel) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And she called his name Joseph; and said, The LORD shall add to me another son
the naming of Joseph and Rachel's hope of another son
Joseph ('he shall add') - the reproach removed (v.23) and the hope of more (Benjamin, 35:18)
the beloved son, future saviour of his house and a type of Christ (chs. 37-50)

schools.summary: Jacob seeks to return to his own country: 'and it came to pass, when Rachel had born Joseph, that Jacob said unto Laban, Send me away, that I may go unto mine own place, and to my country' — after the birth of Joseph (the second seven years being now fulfilled, the fourteen years of service done, vv.20-30), Jacob asking Laban to send him away (the desire to return home, the patriarch longing for the land of promise, 28:15, God's promise to bring him back, the request to depart); Jacob's wish 'to go unto mine own place, and to my country' (back to Canaan, the land of promise, his home, 28:15, the longing of the pilgrim for the land, the providence drawing Jacob homeward, and the time approaching, set by God, the desire to be free of Laban); Jacob's seeking to return marking the turning toward home (fulfilled, ch. 31, the patriarch, his term served and his family grown, longing for Canaan, the providence and the promise, 28:15, drawing him back, the setting for the bargain over wages, vv.26-34) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And it came to pass, when Rachel had born Joseph, that Jacob said unto Laban, Send me away, that I may go unto mine own place, and to my country
Jacob seeking to return to his own country
the longing of the pilgrim for the land of promise (28:15)
the turning toward home, his fourteen years served (ch. 31)

schools.summary: Jacob asks for his wives and children, his service done: 'give me my wives and my children, for whom I have served thee, and let me go: for thou knowest my service which I have done thee' — Jacob asking for his wives and children (whom he had earned by his fourteen years' service, vv.18, 30) and leave to go (the just claim of the patriarch, the service rendered, which Laban well knew, the desire to depart with his family); Jacob having served for his wives (Leah and Rachel, the fourteen years, 29:18-30, the wages duly earned, the just request for his family and freedom); 'thou knowest my service' (Jacob appealing to Laban's own knowledge of his faithful service, the diligent shepherd, vv.29-30; 31:38-40, the honest plea, the service that had prospered Laban, v.27, the setting for the new bargain, vv.27-34) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Give me my wives and my children, for whom I have served thee, and let me go: for thou knowest my service which I have done thee
Jacob asking for his wives and children, his service done
the just claim, the fourteen years' service rendered (29:18-30)
the appeal to Laban's knowledge of his faithful service (31:38)

schools.summary: Laban owns the LORD's blessing for Jacob's sake: 'and Laban said unto him, I pray thee, if I have found favour in thine eyes, tarry: for I have learned by experience that the LORD hath blessed me for thy sake' — Laban urging Jacob to stay (not to depart, the covetous Laban unwilling to lose the prosperer, the request to remain, the setting for the new bargain, vv.28-34); Laban confessing 'I have learned by experience (by divination, or by observation/trial) that the LORD hath blessed me for thy sake' (Laban owning that his prosperity was due to God's blessing on Jacob, the witness even of the covetous Laban to God's blessing on the godly, as the Philistines owned of Isaac, 26:28; Potiphar would of Joseph, 39:3-5, the blessing that flows to the household for the sake of the godly man, the providence acknowledged, whether Laban learned it by trial or by his divinations, the Hebrew may suggest); 'the LORD hath blessed me for thy sake' (God's blessing on Laban for Jacob's sake, the world's witness to the blessing of the godly, the prosperity that the presence of God's servant brings, cf. 39:5, the covetous Laban wishing to keep Jacob for the gain) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Laban said unto him, I pray thee, if I have found favour in thine eyes, tarry: for I have learned by experience that the LORD hath blessed me for thy sake
Laban owning that the LORD had blessed him for Jacob's sake
the world's witness to God's blessing on the godly (26:28; 39:3-5)
the covetous Laban wishing to keep the prosperer

schools.summary: Laban invites Jacob to name his wages: 'and he said, Appoint me thy wages, and I will give it' — Laban inviting Jacob to name his own wages (to keep him, v.27, the seemingly generous offer, the bargain opened, as before, 29:15, the occasion for Jacob's proposal about the speckled cattle, vv.31-33); Laban's invitation — Jacob to set his own wages (the shrewd Laban seeking to retain the prosperer on terms, the bargain that Jacob would frame to his own, and God's, advantage, vv.31-43); 'I will give it' (Laban's promise to pay, the opening of the new arrangement, the setting for Jacob's proposal, the speckled and spotted cattle, v.32, the bargain that would prosper Jacob by God's blessing, 31:9) — Gill.

themes: And he said, Appoint me thy wages, and I will give it
Laban inviting Jacob to name his wages
the new bargain opened (as 29:15), to keep the prosperer
the occasion for Jacob's proposal about the speckled cattle (vv.31-33)

schools.summary: Jacob appeals to his faithful service: 'and he said unto him, Thou knowest how I have served thee, and how thy cattle was with me' — Jacob appealing to Laban's knowledge of his faithful service and how Laban's cattle had fared (prospered) under his keeping (the honest plea of the diligent shepherd, 31:38-40, the service that had increased Laban's flocks, the appeal that grounded the bargain); Jacob's faithful service (the fourteen years, and the care of the flocks, 31:38-40, the diligent, honest shepherd, the service Laban well knew); 'how thy cattle was with me' (Laban's cattle having prospered and multiplied under Jacob's care, the blessing of God on his labor, v.30, the increase Jacob had wrought, the ground for the claim that he should now provide for his own house, v.30) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he said unto him, Thou knowest how I have served thee, and how thy cattle was with me
Jacob appealing to his faithful service
the diligent, honest shepherd (31:38-40)
Laban's cattle prospered under his care, the ground for his claim (v.30)

schools.summary: Jacob notes the increase and his need to provide: 'for it was little which thou hadst before I came, and it is now increased unto a multitude; and the LORD hath blessed thee since my coming: and now when shall I provide for mine own house also?' — Laban's substance, small before Jacob came, having increased greatly under his care (the increase wrought by Jacob's labor, and God's blessing, the prosperity Laban owned, v.27, the great multiplication of the flocks); 'the LORD hath blessed thee since my coming' (Jacob, like Laban, v.27, ascribing the increase to the LORD's blessing, for Jacob's sake, the providence acknowledged, God's blessing on Laban's house for the godly man's sake, the increase of God); 'when shall I provide for mine own house also?' (Jacob noting that, having enriched Laban, it was time he provided for his own family, the lawful care of one's household, 1 Timothy 5:8, the just concern, the ground for the bargain, vv.31-33, the patriarch seeking to build his own estate) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: For it was little which thou hadst before I came, and it is now increased unto a multitude; and the LORD hath blessed thee since my coming: and now when shall I provide for mine own house also?
Jacob noting the increase and his need to provide for his own house
the great increase wrought by God's blessing (for Jacob's sake)
the just concern to provide for one's household (1 Timothy 5:8)

schools.summary: Jacob declines a fixed wage and proposes a condition: 'and he said, What shall I give thee? And Jacob said, Thou shalt not give me any thing: if thou wilt do this thing for me, I will again feed and keep thy flock' — Laban asking what to give, Jacob declining a fixed wage ('thou shalt not give me any thing') — he would take no set hire, but propose a condition (the speckled cattle, v.32, the patriarch's disinterested proposal, leaving his wages to the increase God would give, the refusal of a set wage); Jacob proposing a condition (vv.32-33) — not a wage, but a share of the future increase (the speckled and spotted, which would be his, the bargain that left his prosperity to God's blessing, 31:9, the disinterested terms); 'I will again feed and keep thy flock' (Jacob continuing to shepherd Laban's flock, faithful service, 31:38-40, the offer to keep serving on the proposed terms, the diligent shepherd, the bargain that would, by God's blessing, prosper Jacob, vv.37-43) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he said, What shall I give thee? And Jacob said, Thou shalt not give me any thing: if thou wilt do this thing for me, I will again feed and keep thy flock
Jacob declining a fixed wage and proposing a condition
the disinterested terms, leaving his prosperity to the increase God would give (31:9)
the continued faithful service of the shepherd (31:38-40)

schools.summary: Jacob's proposal of the speckled and spotted as his hire: 'I will pass through all thy flock to day, removing from thence all the speckled and spotted cattle, and all the brown cattle among the sheep, and the spotted and speckled among the goats: and of such shall be my hire' — Jacob proposing that the off-colored animals (the speckled, spotted, and brown — the unusual, less common colors, the sheep being mostly white and the goats mostly black/dark) should be his wages (the proposal that gave Laban the advantage, the common colors his, the rare ones Jacob's, the disinterested, modest terms); the speckled, spotted, and brown the less usual colors (the few among the flock, Jacob asking only these, the modest share, the bargain that seemed to favor Laban, yet God would prosper Jacob, 31:9); 'of such shall be my hire' (the off-colored animals, present and future, to be Jacob's wages, the terms set, the bargain that left Jacob's prosperity to the increase of the rare colors, which God would multiply, vv.37-43; 31:9, the disinterested proposal) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: I will pass through all thy flock to day, removing from thence all the speckled and spotted cattle... and of such shall be my hire
Jacob's proposal of the speckled and spotted as his hire
the off-colored (rare) animals as wages, seemingly to Laban's advantage
his prosperity left to the increase God would give (31:9; vv.37-43)

schools.summary: Jacob appeals to his righteousness, the marks proving his honesty: 'so shall my righteousness answer for me in time to come, when it shall come for my hire before thy face: every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats, and brown among the sheep, that shall be counted stolen with me' — Jacob's proposal would prove his honesty: the easily distinguished off-colored animals (his) could not be confused with the common-colored (Laban's, his righteousness, integrity, would be evident, the bargain framed to put his honesty beyond dispute, the upright dealing of the patriarch); the test — any common-colored animal found among Jacob's flock would be counted stolen (proof of theft, the clear mark distinguishing his from Laban's, the bargain that left no room for dispute or suspicion, the honest, transparent terms, the rare colors his, the rest Laban's); 'when it shall come for my hire before thy face' (Jacob's wages open to Laban's inspection at any time, the transparency, the integrity that courted examination, the upright dealing, the contrast with Laban's fraud, 29:25; 31:7, the honest patriarch) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: So shall my righteousness answer for me in time to come... every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats, and brown among the sheep, that shall be counted stolen with me
Jacob appealing to his righteousness, the marks proving his honesty
the clear marks distinguishing his from Laban's, integrity beyond dispute
the transparent dealing (the contrast with Laban's fraud, 29:25; 31:7)

schools.summary: Laban accepts Jacob's terms: 'and Laban said, Behold, I would it might be according to thy word' — Laban readily agreeing to Jacob's proposal (vv.31-33, the covetous Laban, thinking the terms to his advantage, the rare colors few, the common many, gladly consenting, the bargain struck, the agreement that Laban thought favored him, yet God would prosper Jacob, 31:9); Laban's eager consent — the terms seeming to his gain (the speckled and spotted being few, the covetous man's quick agreement, the bargain accepted); Laban's consent (thinking to profit) setting the stage for God's overruling — the off-colored increasing greatly (vv.37-43), so that Jacob (not Laban) prospered (the providence turning the covetous bargain to Jacob's gain, 31:9, God took the cattle and gave them to Jacob, the bargain that God would bless) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Laban said, Behold, I would it might be according to thy word
Laban accepting Jacob's terms
the covetous Laban consenting, thinking the terms to his gain
the bargain God would turn to Jacob's prosperity (31:9)

schools.summary: Laban removes the off-colored cattle to his sons: 'and he removed that day the he goats that were ringstraked and spotted, and all the she goats that were speckled and spotted, and every one that had some white in it, and all the brown among the sheep, and gave them into the hand of his sons' — Laban (or, by the Hebrew, Jacob acting, but most take Laban) removing all the off-colored animals (the ringstraked, spotted, speckled, brown — Jacob's wages by the bargain, v.32) from the flock (the separation that Laban made to his own advantage, taking away the present off-colored, leaving Jacob only the future increase); Laban 'gave them into the hand of his sons' (putting the off-colored, Jacob's by right, into the care of his own sons, far off, v.36, the covetous, suspicious Laban removing the speckled stock, lest Jacob profit by them, the unfair dealing, Laban already begrudging Jacob his wages); Laban's removing the off-colored putting Jacob at a seeming disadvantage (only the common-colored, mostly white and dark, left, from which the off-colored must come, yet the providence would multiply the off-colored, vv.37-43; 31:9, prospering Jacob despite Laban's craft, the covetous separation overruled by God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And he removed that day the he goats that were ringstraked and spotted... and all the brown among the sheep, and gave them into the hand of his sons
Laban removing the off-colored cattle to his sons
the covetous craft, putting Jacob at a seeming disadvantage
the craft God would overrule to prosper Jacob (vv.37-43; 31:9)

schools.summary: The three days' journey set between the flocks: 'and he set three days' journey betwixt himself and Jacob: and Jacob fed the rest of Laban's flocks' — Laban putting a three days' journey between his (the off-colored, with his sons) flock and Jacob's (the common-colored, the separation to keep the off-colored far from Jacob, lest the speckled breed with the common, the covetous precaution, Jacob left to feed the common-colored remainder); the three days' journey — the wide separation (Laban's care to keep the off-colored stock away from Jacob, the precaution against Jacob's profit); 'Jacob fed the rest of Laban's flocks' (Jacob shepherding the common-colored, white sheep, dark goats, left to him, the flock from which his wages, the off-colored, must come, the setting for the device of the rods, vv.37-43, the providence that would multiply the off-colored despite the disadvantage, 31:9) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he set three days' journey betwixt himself and Jacob: and Jacob fed the rest of Laban's flocks
the three days' journey set between the flocks
Laban's covetous precaution, the off-colored kept far off
Jacob left with the common-colored, the setting for the device of the rods (vv.37-43)

schools.summary: Jacob's device of the peeled rods: 'and Jacob took him rods of green poplar, and of the hazel and chesnut tree; and pilled white strakes in them, and made the white appear which was in the rods' — Jacob taking fresh rods of poplar, hazel (almond), and plane (chestnut), and peeling white streaks in them (laying bare the white wood under the bark), making them appear partly-colored (white and dark, the device he would set before the flocks at watering, v.38, that they might conceive partly-colored young, v.39); the dominant reading holding that Jacob's plan rested on the notion (held in antiquity) that what the breeding animals looked upon (the partly-colored rods) would, by the power of imagination (the maternal impression), affect the color of their offspring — making them bring forth ringstraked, speckled, and spotted young (v.39, whether this natural means had any real efficacy, some defending a real effect, many doubting it, the device Jacob's own contrivance); whatever the device (and whatever its natural efficacy, real or merely supposed), the real cause of Jacob's prospering was God's blessing — for Jacob himself afterward declared that it was God who took away Laban's cattle and gave them to him, the Angel showing him in a dream that the rams which leaped were ringstraked (31:9-12, God overruling, and perhaps using or transcending the means, to multiply the off-colored and prosper Jacob, requiting Laban's fraud, the increase ascribed ultimately to God, not the rods, the witnesses warning against trusting the device rather than God, though Jacob's faith looked to God's promise, 31:13) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, JFB, Henry.

debates.question: By what did Jacob's flocks bring forth the speckled - the device, or God's blessing?

sides.position: The rods working (it was supposed) by the power of imagination on the breeding animals (the maternal-impression notion held in antiquity) - whether of real efficacy or not, Jacob's own contrivance
The real cause was God's blessing - Jacob owned that God took the cattle and gave them to him (31:9-12), God overruling the means to prosper him and requite Laban's fraud

themes: And Jacob took him rods of green poplar, and of the hazel and chesnut tree; and pilled white strakes in them, and made the white appear which was in the rods
Jacob's device of the peeled rods (the crux of the device and God's blessing)
the rods working (it was supposed) by the power of imagination on the breeding animals
the real cause God's blessing - God took the cattle and gave them to Jacob (31:9-12)

schools.summary: Jacob sets the rods at the watering troughs: 'and he set the rods which he had pilled before the flocks in the gutters in the watering troughs when the flocks came to drink, that they should conceive when they came to drink' — Jacob placing the peeled, partly-colored rods (v.37) in the watering troughs, before the flocks (the device set at the place of watering, where the flocks gathered and bred, the rods before the animals' eyes); the flocks coming to drink and there conceiving — the rods set so that the breeding animals would look on the partly-colored rods (the device, by the supposed power of imagination, v.37, to make them bear partly-colored young, the placing at the time of conception, the contrivance applied); the rods set at conception — Jacob's means, yet the increase of the off-colored (v.39) the work of God's blessing (31:9-12, the device applied, the result God's gift, the providence prospering Jacob) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And he set the rods which he had pilled before the flocks in the gutters in the watering troughs when the flocks came to drink, that they should conceive when they came to drink
Jacob setting the rods at the watering troughs
the device set at the place and time of breeding
the increase that followed God's blessing (31:9-12)

schools.summary: The flocks bring forth partly-colored young: 'and the flocks conceived before the rods, and brought forth cattle ringstraked, speckled, and spotted' — the flocks, breeding before the rods (v.38), bringing forth partly-colored young (ringstraked, speckled, spotted — Jacob's by the bargain, v.32, the increase of the off-colored, the device followed by the result, the multiplying of Jacob's wages); the breeding before the rods (the device, v.37) followed by the partly-colored offspring (whether by the power of imagination, the supposed natural means, or, the truer cause, by God's blessing, 31:9-12, the increase that followed); 'brought forth cattle ringstraked, speckled, and spotted' (the off-colored young, Jacob's wages, v.32, the multiplying of the rare colors, the prosperity of Jacob, by God's blessing, 31:9, the increase that built his estate, v.43) — Gill.

themes: And the flocks conceived before the rods, and brought forth cattle ringstraked, speckled, and spotted
the flocks bringing forth partly-colored young
the off-colored multiplying for Jacob (his wages, v.32)
the increase by God's blessing (31:9)

schools.summary: Jacob separates his flocks from Laban's: 'and Jacob did separate the lambs, and set the faces of the flocks toward the ringstraked, and all the brown in the flock of Laban; and he put his own flocks by themselves, and put them not unto Laban's cattle' — Jacob, as the off-colored increased (v.39), separating them and setting the faces of the breeding flock toward the ringstraked and brown (so that they looked on the off-colored, a further device, with the rods, to multiply the partly-colored, the careful management of the breeding); Jacob 'put his own flocks by themselves, and put them not unto Laban's cattle' (Jacob keeping his, the off-colored, separate from Laban's, the common-colored, the careful keeping of his own increasing flock distinct, the honest separation, his marked off from Laban's, the prospering of his own estate); Jacob's careful breeding-management (the rods, the facing toward the off-colored) his means, yet the increase God's blessing (31:9-12, the prospering of Jacob's own flock, the providence building his estate, v.43, the diligent shepherd blessed of God) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: And Jacob did separate the lambs, and set the faces of the flocks toward the ringstraked... and he put his own flocks by themselves, and put them not unto Laban's cattle
Jacob separating his flocks from Laban's
the careful breeding-management, keeping his own distinct
the increase God's blessing (31:9), the prospering of his estate

schools.summary: Jacob uses the rods for the stronger cattle: 'and it came to pass, whensoever the stronger cattle did conceive, that Jacob laid the rods before the eyes of the cattle in the gutters, that they might conceive among the rods' — Jacob laying the rods before the stronger (more vigorous) cattle when they bred, so that the stronger young would be off-colored (his, the selective use of the device for the stronger, the careful management to gain the best of the increase); Jacob applying the rods only to the stronger (the vigorous) — so his flock (the off-colored) would be the strongest (the prudent management, the best of the increase to Jacob); the rods before the stronger — Jacob's contrivance, yet the increase (and the strength of his flock) the work of God's blessing (31:9-12, the providence prospering Jacob with the best, the diligent shepherd's management blessed of God) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And it came to pass, whensoever the stronger cattle did conceive, that Jacob laid the rods before the eyes of the cattle in the gutters, that they might conceive among the rods
Jacob using the rods for the stronger cattle
the selective management to gain the best of the increase
the increase and strength of his flock God's blessing (31:9)

schools.summary: The feebler left to Laban, the stronger to Jacob: 'but when the cattle were feeble, he put them not in: so the feebler were Laban's, and the stronger Jacob's' — Jacob not using the rods for the feeble (weaker) cattle, so the feebler (common-colored) were Laban's, and the stronger (off-colored) Jacob's (the prudent management, the strong flock to Jacob, the weak to Laban, the prospering of Jacob's estate); Jacob withholding the rods from the feeble — so they bred common-colored (Laban's, the careful selection, the strong to Jacob, the weak to Laban); the result — Jacob's flock strong and increasing, Laban's feeble (the prospering of Jacob, by God's blessing, 31:9, the requital of Laban's fraud, he had cheated Jacob, 29:25; 31:7, and now his flock dwindled, the providence building Jacob's estate, v.43) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: But when the cattle were feeble, he put them not in: so the feebler were Laban's, and the stronger Jacob's
the feebler left to Laban, the stronger to Jacob
the strong flock to Jacob (by God's blessing, 31:9)
the requital of Laban's fraud (29:25; 31:7)

schools.summary: Jacob's great increase and prosperity: 'and the man increased exceedingly, and had much cattle, and maidservants, and menservants, and camels, and asses' — Jacob increasing greatly (exceedingly), gaining much cattle (and, by trading his cattle, servants, camels, and asses, the great prosperity of the patriarch, the wealth God gave him, 31:9, the building of his estate from the off-colored flocks, vv.37-42); Jacob's great wealth — much cattle and a large household (servants, camels, asses, the prospering of the patriarch, as Abraham and Isaac before him, 24:35; 26:13, the blessing of God making him rich); 'the man increased exceedingly' (the great increase, the work of God's blessing, 31:9-12, God took Laban's cattle and gave them to Jacob, the requital of Laban, whose flocks dwindled, v.42, and the enriching of Jacob, the providence prospering the patriarch, the wealth that would set the stage for his return to Canaan, ch. 31) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, JFB.

themes: And the man increased exceedingly, and had much cattle, and maidservants, and menservants, and camels, and asses
Jacob's great increase and prosperity
the patriarch enriched by God's blessing (31:9; as 24:35; 26:13)
the wealth that would set the stage for his return to Canaan (ch. 31)
